briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the climate briefing for March 11, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensification of Global Climate Threats and Impacts
sections.content: Today's climate news warns that abnormal phenomena caused by climate change are becoming increasingly evident worldwide. Domestically, 'unprecedented' heavy rainfall has occurred, hinting at the future of climate change [2], and the observation of holes forming in the Arctic ice sheet is being identified as a 'hidden variable' of climate change [3, 68]. Internationally, climate change is also causing serious concern, with North Carolina's allergy season lengthening [178] and birds returning to Minnesota earlier than usual [180], indicating ecological shifts. These phenomena demonstrate that climate change is not merely an environmental issue but has widespread impacts across our daily lives and natural ecosystems [192].

sections.title: 🌱 Strengthening Corporate Leadership in Climate Change Response
sections.content: There is much positive news indicating that domestic companies are playing a leading role in responding to climate change. Hyundai Engineering & Construction has demonstrated its carbon neutrality achievements by being inducted into the 'Hall of Fame' for eight consecutive years in the CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project) Climate Change category [4, 22, 23, 34, 38, 41, 49, 65, 69, 72, 79, 81, 82, 86, 93], and SK Hynix also achieved a double crown, ranking first for 13 consecutive years in the CDP Climate Change category and entering the 'Hall of Fame,' while also excelling in water resource management [10, 11, 14, 27, 28, 30, 32, 33, 35, 37, 40, 48, 55, 59, 60, 61, 75, 77, 181]. Furthermore, numerous companies including KT&G, Hanwha Corp., Hyundai Glovis, LG Innotek, KB Financial Group, and Industrial Bank of Korea were recognized for their climate change response efforts, being selected as excellent companies or achieving 'Leadership Level' in the CDP evaluation [5, 6, 7, 12, 13, 16, 17, 18, 20, 24, 26, 39, 42, 43, 44, 45, 47, 53, 54, 56, 57, 58, 62, 63, 64, 66, 67, 71, 73, 74, 83, 84, 91, 100]. The fact that 91% of CDP participating companies manage climate change at the board level indicates the deepening integration of ESG management within corporations [8].

sections.title: 🌍 International Cooperation and Policy Trends
sections.content: International cooperation and policy efforts to address climate change are actively underway. The Republic of Korea has signed a climate change cooperation agreement with Ghana and agreed to strengthen cooperation in maritime security and digital sectors [1, 9, 15, 21, 25, 29, 31, 36, 46, 52, 76, 78, 80, 170, 193, 194]. This exemplifies recognizing climate change as a complex international issue and seeking multifaceted approaches. Meanwhile, the UK government's climate advisory group analyzed that achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 would cost less than a single oil crisis [173, 200, 201], emphasizing the economic benefits of achieving net-zero. New York State has decided to mandate climate change education [176], and Greece has unveiled a strategy to protect cultural heritage from climate change [187], demonstrating how various countries are pursuing climate policies tailored to their specific characteristics.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition Efforts and Challenges
sections.content: Domestically, efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society and expand renewable energy are underway in various sectors, but simultaneously face several challenges. The government is promoting the establishment of a 'Basic Plan for Industrial Transition and Employment Stability' to respond to industrial structural changes resulting from the spread of AI and the carbon-neutral transition [90, 95, 104, 108, 123, 172], and the Korea Environment Corporation is pursuing the development of an AI-powered carbon emission management system through public-private cooperation [96, 97, 98, 111]. In local communities, various citizen-participatory carbon neutrality practices are continuing, such as Gyeryong City's recruitment for the 'Carbon Neutrality Point System for Vehicles' [87, 101, 109, 113, 115, 124] and Muan County's youth carbon neutrality program [88, 94, 116]. In the renewable energy sector, Jeollanam-do Province is accelerating the creation of public-led power generation complexes [129, 164], and Gokseong County is building a 'citizen-participatory renewable energy model' where residents invest and share profits [125, 139, 150, 154, 155, 163, 167], demonstrating innovative attempts. However, criticism has been raised that the current RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standard) reform proposal is insufficient to achieve the government's renewable energy targets [131, 132, 135], and the reality that Chungcheongbuk-do Province's electricity self-sufficiency rate is only 10% further highlights the need for renewable energy expansion [151]. Amidst the energy crisis originating from the Middle East, Climate Minister Kim Sung-hwan emphasized that the transition to renewable energy is key to energy security and called for accelerating this major transition [127, 141, 156, 165].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the intensifying climate threats and the diverse efforts of businesses, nations, and local communities to combat them. Through continuous international cooperation and the implementation of domestic policies, we must accelerate the transition to a carbon-neutral society. This concludes the climate briefing for March 11, 2026.
